new_obligation_classes.label: Confidentiality Obligation
new_obligation_classes.definition: A professional duty to protect confidential client information and to share it only with the prior consent of the client, including obtaining permission before placing client information in a public domain such as an open-source AI interface.
text_references: Per Code section II.1.c, confidential information can only be shared with prior consent of the Client.
The facts here do not indicate Engineer A obtained permission from Client W to use the private information in the public domain.
new_obligation_classes.source_text: Per Code section II.1.c, confidential information can only be shared with prior consent of the Client. While careful review and checking of AI-generated content was consistent for ethical use, this does not end the inquiry into Engineer A's actions.

match_decision.reasoning: The Board invokes the duty to protect confidential client information and obtain prior client consent before sharing it, which is exactly the existing Confidentiality Obligation class (NSPE III.4 / II.1.c). The AI upload context is case-specific and belongs on the individual, per the synonym rule mapping Client Data Upload Consent Obligation to ConfidentialityObligation.

new_obligation_classes.label: Attribution Obligation
new_obligation_classes.definition: A professional duty to give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due and to recognize the proprietary interests of others, including appropriate citation of pertinent documents of technical authority in tool-assisted work.
text_references: Per Code section III.9, engineers are required to "give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due," so Engineer A's ethical use of the AI software would need to include appropriate citations.
new_obligation_classes.source_text: Similarly, the facts do not indicate the AI-generated report included citations of pertinent documents of technical authority. Per Code section III.9, engineers are required to "give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due," so Engineer A's ethical use of the AI software would need to include appropriate citations.

match_decision.reasoning: The Board's NSPE III.9 credit-and-citation duty is the same concept as the existing Attribution Obligation class, which explicitly covers citation of documents of technical authority and credit for AI-assisted content.

new_obligation_classes.label: Competence Obligation
new_obligation_classes.definition: A professional duty to perform services only in areas of one's competence and to undertake assignments only when qualified by education or experience in the specific technical fields involved.
text_references: Fundamental Canon I.2 states that engineers "perform services only in areas of their competence" and Code section II.2.a states that engineers must "undertake assignments only when qualified by education or experience in the specific technical fields involved."
new_obligation_classes.source_text: Fundamental Canon I.2 states that engineers "perform services only in areas of their competence" and Code section II.2.a states that engineers must "undertake assignments only when qualified by education or experience in the specific technical fields involved." Here, Engineer A, as an experienced environmental engineer, was competent to analyze groundwater monitoring data and assess contaminant risks.

match_decision.reasoning: The Board directly invokes NSPE I.2 and II.2.a, the requirement to perform services only in areas of competence, which is the existing Competence Obligation class.

new_obligation_classes.label: Safety Obligation
new_obligation_classes.definition: A professional duty to hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public in the performance of professional duties, including ensuring that design deliverables do not create safety hazards or regulatory noncompliance.
text_references: The errors in the AI-generated design documents could have led to regulatory noncompliance and safety hazards, conflicting with the Fundamental Canon I.1, "hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public".
new_obligation_classes.source_text: The errors in the AI-generated design documents could have led to regulatory noncompliance and safety hazards, conflicting with the Fundamental Canon I.1, "hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public".

match_decision.reasoning: The Board invokes Fundamental Canon I.1, the paramount public safety duty, which is exactly the existing Safety Obligation class (NSPE I.1).

text_references: Per Code section II.1.c, confidential information can only be shared with prior consent of the Client.
The facts here do not indicate Engineer A obtained permission from Client W to use the private information in the public domain.
obligation_individuals.source_text: When Engineer A uploaded Client W's information into the AI open-source interface, this was tantamount to placing the Client's private information in the public domain. The facts here do not indicate Engineer A obtained permission from Client W to use the private information in the public domain.

match_decision.reasoning: This duty instantiates the canonical Confidentiality Obligation, with the AI upload as case-specific context on the individual.

obligation_individuals.obligation_statement: Engineer A shall obtain Client W's prior consent before sharing Client W's confidential information, including before uploading it into the AI open-source interface where it is tantamount to placing it in the public domain.
obligation_individuals.case_context: Engineer A uploaded Client W's information into the AI open-source interface without any indication of prior permission from Client W, which the Board treats as placing the client's private information in the public domain.
obligation_individuals.temporal_scope: Before sharing or uploading Client W's confidential information to any public-facing tool or interface

text_references: Per Code section III.9, engineers are required to "give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due," so Engineer A's ethical use of the AI software would need to include appropriate citations.
the facts do not indicate the AI-generated report included citations of pertinent documents of technical authority
obligation_individuals.source_text: Similarly, the facts do not indicate the AI-generated report included citations of pertinent documents of technical authority. Per Code section III.9, engineers are required to "give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due," so Engineer A's ethical use of the AI software would need to include appropriate citations.

match_decision.reasoning: This duty instantiates the canonical Attribution Obligation, the NSPE III.9 credit duty, applied here to citations of documents of technical authority in the AI-generated report.

obligation_individuals.obligation_statement: Engineer A shall give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due, including appropriate citations of pertinent documents of technical authority in the AI-generated report.
obligation_individuals.case_context: The AI-generated report lacked citations of pertinent documents of technical authority, and the Board holds that ethical use of the AI software would need to include appropriate citations; absent this level of care, diligence, and documentation, Engineer A's use of the AI language processing software would be less than ethical.
obligation_individuals.temporal_scope: When preparing and delivering the AI-assisted report to Client W

text_references: Here, Engineer A, as an experienced environmental engineer, was competent to analyze groundwater monitoring data and assess contaminant risks.
it is the BER's view that under the facts, unlike the situation of BER Case 98-3 , Engineer A is not incompetent
obligation_individuals.source_text: Fundamental Canon I.2 states that engineers "perform services only in areas of their competence" and Code section II.2.a states that engineers must "undertake assignments only when qualified by education or experience in the specific technical fields involved." Here, Engineer A, as an experienced environmental engineer, was competent to analyze groundwater monitoring data and assess contaminant risks.

match_decision.reasoning: This duty instantiates the canonical Competence Obligation (NSPE I.2 and II.2.a), which the Board finds Engineer A satisfied.

obligation_individuals.obligation_statement: Engineer A shall perform services only in areas of their competence and undertake the groundwater monitoring analysis and contaminant risk assessment only as qualified by education or experience.
obligation_individuals.case_context: The Board finds Engineer A, an experienced environmental engineer with several years of experience and strong technical expertise, was competent to analyze groundwater monitoring data and assess contaminant risks, and explicitly holds that Engineer A is not incompetent.
obligation_individuals.temporal_scope: Throughout the engagement with Client W

text_references: The errors in the AI-generated design documents could have led to regulatory noncompliance and safety hazards, conflicting with the Fundamental Canon I.1, "hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public".
the identified errors suggest insufficient review, which could compromise public welfare and impinge on Engineer A's ethical and professional obligations
obligation_individuals.source_text: By relying on AI-assisted tools without a comprehensive verification process of its output, Engineer A risked violating this requirement. Furthermore, failure to detect misaligned dimensions and omitted safety features further indicates that Engineer A did not exercise sufficient diligence. The errors in the AI-generated design documents could have led to regulatory noncompliance and safety hazards, conflicting with the Fundamental Canon I.1, "hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public".

match_decision.reasoning: This duty instantiates the canonical Safety Obligation (NSPE I.1), which the Board finds Engineer A's insufficiently reviewed design documents conflicted with.

obligation_individuals.obligation_statement: Engineer A shall hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public in preparing the engineering design documents, ensuring they do not omit required safety features or create safety hazards.
obligation_individuals.case_context: The AI-generated design documents contained misaligned dimensions and omitted key safety features necessary for compliance with local regulations, and the Board finds these errors could have led to regulatory noncompliance and safety hazards conflicting with Fundamental Canon I.1.
obligation_individuals.temporal_scope: During preparation, review, and delivery of the design documents for the groundwater infrastructure modifications
